354	\begin{lemma}[The inclusion and projection are chain maps]
355	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}
356	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSplitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_shortExact,
357	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeMid, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES,
358	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_splitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι,
359	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι_f_zero, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.mono_horseshoeβ,
360	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod_X, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod_d,
361	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_fst, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_snd,
362	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd}
363	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
364	  With \(d_B\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, the degreewise biproduct
365	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
366	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
367	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that
368	  is, \(d_B^n \circ \iota^n = \iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\) and
369	  \(\pi^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = d_C^n \circ \pi^n\). Moreover \(\iota, \pi\) are compatible
370	  with the augmentations and form, in each degree, the split short exact sequence
371	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\) of
372	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
373	\end{lemma}
374	
375	\begin{proof}
376	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp, lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
377	  These are direct biproduct computations from the matrix form of \(d_B\). Pre-composing
378	  \(d_B^n\) with the coprojection \(\iota^n\) of the first summand selects the first
379	  column \(\begin{pmatrix} d_A^n \\ 0\end{pmatrix}\), which equals
380	  \(\iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\); so \(\iota\) is a chain map. Post-composing \(d_B^n\) with
381	  the projection \(\pi^{n+1}\) onto the second summand selects the second row
382	  \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 & d_C^n\end{pmatrix}\), which equals \(d_C^n \circ \pi^n\); so
383	  \(\pi\) is a chain map. Compatibility with the augmentations is the corresponding
384	  statement in degree \(-1 \to 0\), immediate from the construction of \(\beta\) in
385	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. In each degree \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n}\) and
386	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n}\) are the structure maps of the biproduct, so the degree is split
387	  exact by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
388	\end{proof}

390	\begin{lemma}\leanok
391	[Middle-term quasi-isomorphism transfer]
392	  \label{lem:quasiIso_tau2}
393	  \lean{HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.quasiIso_τ₂}
394	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
395	  Project-local supplement (the \(\tau_2\) companion of Mathlib's \(\tau_3\) transfer
396	  \texttt{quasiIso\_τ₃}). Let \(\varphi : S_1 \to S_2\) be a morphism of short exact
397	  sequences of cochain complexes, with components \(\varphi.\tau_1, \varphi.\tau_2,
398	  \varphi.\tau_3\) on the three columns. Suppose \(\varphi.\tau_1\) and \(\varphi.\tau_3\)
399	  are quasi-isomorphisms, and that the middle component \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a
400	  monomorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no incoming differential, and an
401	  epimorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no outgoing differential. Then
402	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
403	\end{lemma}
404	
405	\begin{proof}
406	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
407	  \leanok
408	  This is the homology four-lemma. Fix a degree \(i\); we show
409	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism by proving it is both epimorphic and
410	  monomorphic and concluding that a map which is both is an isomorphism. \emph{Epi:}
411	  when \(i\) has an outgoing differential to some \(j\), the two short exact sequences
412	  yield a commuting ladder whose rows are the five-term exact windows of the homology
413	  long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) about degree
414	  \(i\); the four-lemma (epi form), using that \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1)\),
415	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are epi and \(H^j(\varphi.\tau_1)\) is mono, forces
416	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) epi. When \(i\) has no outgoing differential,
417	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is epi in degree \(i\) by hypothesis, and an epimorphism of
418	  complexes that is epi in a degree with no outgoing differential is epi on homology
419	  there. \emph{Mono:} dually, when \(i\) has an incoming differential from some \(k\),
420	  the four-lemma (mono form), using that \(H^k(\varphi.\tau_3)\) is epi and
421	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1), H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are mono, forces
422	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) mono; when \(i\) has no incoming differential it is mono in
423	  that degree by hypothesis, hence mono on homology. Being epi and mono in every
424	  degree, \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), so
425	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
426	\end{proof}

428	\begin{lemma}[The middle complex resolves \(B\)]
429	  \label{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}
430	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle,
431	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₁₂,
432	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₂₃, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₁,
433	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₂, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₃,
434	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.quasiIso_horseshoeι, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.single₀_hom_ext}
435	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
436	  The cochain complex \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, with the
437	  augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, is an injective
438	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective
439	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}), it is exact in every positive degree, and
440	  \(H^0 \cong B\) via \(\beta\).
441	\end{lemma}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
445	    lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
446	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap} the maps \(\iota, \pi\) form, degreewise, the
447	  split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\); assembling these
448	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of
449	  cochain complexes
450	  \[
451	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
452	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .
453	  \]
454	  Apply the complex-level homology long exact sequence
455	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),
456	  \(I_C^\bullet\) are injective resolutions, hence exact in every positive degree
457	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),
458	  \(H^0(I_C) \cong C\). In the long exact sequence the terms flanking \(H^{k}(I_B)\) for
459	  \(k \geq 1\) vanish, forcing \(H^{k}(I_B) = 0\); in degree \(0\) the initial segment
460	  \(0 \to H^0(I_A) \to H^0(I_B) \to H^0(I_C) \to H^1(I_A) = 0\) reads
461	  \(0 \to A \to H^0(I_B) \to C \to 0\), which together with the original sequence
462	  \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\) and the augmentation \(\beta\) identifies
463	  \(H^0(I_B) \cong B\). Hence \(I_B^\bullet\) is exact in positive degrees and resolves
464	  \(B\); with injective terms it is an injective resolution of \(B\).
465	\end{proof}

467	\begin{lemma}\leanok
468	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
469	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
470	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
471	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
472	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
473	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
474	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
475	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
476	  \[
477	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
478	  \]
479	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
480	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
481	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
482	  \[
483	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
484	  \]
485	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
486	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
487	  \[
488	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
489	      \longrightarrow 0
490	  \]
491	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
492	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
493	\end{lemma}
494	
495	\begin{proof}
496	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
497	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
498	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
499	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
500	  \leanok
501	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
502	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
503	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
504	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
506	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
508	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
509	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
510	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted
511	  degreewise-split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A \to I_B \to I_C \to 0\) of cochain
512	  complexes compatible with the augmentations.
513	\end{proof}

515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
530	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES, CategoryTheory.shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic}
531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
560	\end{proof}
561	
562	\begin{lemma}\leanok
563	[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
564	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
565	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
566	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
567	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
568	    lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
569	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
570	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
571	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
573	  % between abelian categories and assume
574	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
575	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
576	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
577	  % \begin{enumerate}
578	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
579	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
580	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
581	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
582	  % \end{enumerate}
583	  % In each of the three cases
584	  % $$
585	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
586	  % $$
587	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
588	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
589	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
590	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
591	  \[
592	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
593	  \]
594	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
595	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of
596	  right-derived functors furnish isomorphisms
597	  \[
598	    (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
599	    \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
600	  \]
601	  (The companion lowest-degree statement
602	  \((R^{1} G)(A) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J) \to G(Z))\) is isolated as the
603	  sibling Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
607	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
608	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
609	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
611	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
612	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
613	\begin{proof}
614	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
615	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
616	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
617	    \leanok
618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
622	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
623	
624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes
628	  \[
629	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
630	  \]
631	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
633	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
635	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
637	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
638	  \[
639	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
640	      \longrightarrow 0
641	  \]
642	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
643	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
644	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
645	  \[
646	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]
650	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
652	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
653	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
654	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[
657	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
659	  \]
660	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
661	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
662	  \[
663	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
664	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
665	  \]
666	
667	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
668	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
669	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
670	  \[
671	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
672	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
673	  \]
674	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
675	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
676	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
677	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
678	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
679	\end{proof}
680	
681	\begin{lemma}\leanok
682	[Lowest-degree cokernel description across an acyclic short exact sequence]
683	  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}
684	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}
685	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
686	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
687	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
688	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
689	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
690	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
692	  % between abelian categories and assume
693	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
694	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
695	  % of $\mathcal{A}$."
696	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
697	  % $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
698	  % (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
699	  % we obtain an exact cohomology sequence

702	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
703	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
704	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
705	  \[
706	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
707	  \]
708	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
709	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
710	  \[
711	    (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
712	  \]
713	  i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by the
714	  surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
715	\end{lemma}
716	
717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
719	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
720	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
721	    \leanok
722	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
723	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
724	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
726	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
727	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
728	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
729	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
730	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
731	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
732	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
734	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
736	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
737	  initial segment
738	  \[
739	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
740	  \]
741	  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
742	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
743	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing
744	  right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is the image of
745	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism
746	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \cong\ } (R^1 G)(A)\),
747	  which is the assertion.
748	\end{proof}

750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}
864	
865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

961	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
962	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
963	  Let

966	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2

990	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
991	% the long exact cohomology sequence
992	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
993	% to an exact sequence
994	% $$
995	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
996	% $$
997	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
998	\begin{proof}
999	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
1000	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
1001	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
1002	    \leanok
1003	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.
1008	
1009	  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013	
1014	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]
1026	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1027	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1028	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1029	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1030	  acyclic:
1031	  \[
1032	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
1033	  \]
1034	  Finally Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution} identifies this cokernel
1035	  with the cohomology of the applied complex,
1036	  \(\operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\). Composing
1037	  the staircase, the cokernel description, and this identification gives
1038	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for every \(n \geq 1\); together with the
1039	  degree-zero case this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
1040

1154	\begin{proof}

1156	  \leanok
1157	  Exactness of \(D^\bullet\) at each positive-degree node is a local-to-global
1158	  statement over the spanning family. By the local-to-global exactness criterion
1159	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\) (with the
1160	  spanning hypothesis \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\)), it
1161	  suffices to verify \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p+1}, d^{p+2})\) after
1162	  localising at each spanning element \(s_r\). In the localisation at \(s_r\) the
1163	  comparison family \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\) identifies the localised
1164	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) with the localised Čech differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1165	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} (using the localisation
1166	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\) of
1167	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\)). Exactness of that

1172	  giving the localised exactness one localisation at a time. Reassembling over the

1298	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) of the underlying localisation maps. Equivalently,
1299	  the extended complex \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{s_{i_0}} \to \cdots\) (whose
1300	  truncation is studied in Algebra, Lemma \texttt{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is
1301	  exact in positive degrees. The active target is the tilde case
1302	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), where the section identification of
1303	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} is gap-free and the coface match is
1304	  the abstract unfold of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} composed with the
1305	  tilde bridge of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}; a general quasi-coherent
1306	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case by \(\mathcal{F} \cong
1307	  \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the separately-deferred
1308	  globalisation gap.
1309	
1310	  \paragraph{Node-by-node exactness via local-to-global and the dependent port.}
1311	  Exactness of each positive-degree node is established from the spanning family
1312	  \((s, hs)\). The local-to-global exactness criterion
1313	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\, hs\)
1314	  reduces \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p-1}, d^{p})\) to exactness after
1315	  localising at each spanning element \(r\) (the away localisation
1316	  \(\operatorname{Away}(s_r)\)), checking exactness one localisation at a time. In the
1317	  localisation at \(s_r\) the fixed index \(i_{\mathrm{fix}} = r\) makes \(s_r\)
1318	  invertible, so the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) transport \(c\)
1319	  (\(\operatorname{depTransport}\)) is defined globally on that localisation and
1320	  furnishes a contracting homotopy of the localised complex: the unit, shift, and
1321	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\) (\(c \circ \delta_0 =
1322	  \operatorname{id}\)), \(\mathtt{hsh}\) (\(c \circ \delta_{k+1} = \delta_k \circ c\)),
1323	  and \(\mathtt{hcomm}\) hold for the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\). The node is
1324	  then closed by \(\operatorname{depDiff\_exact}\) of the dependent combinatorial port,
1325	  which packages the identity \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\) into the
1326	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\) statement that
1327	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}\) consumes. The constant- and
1328	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial cores and the spanning-family certifier are
1329	  already in place; the section and differential identifications of
1330	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and the homology--exactness reduction of
1331	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} are the bridge that connects them to the
1332	  abstract section complex.
1333	
1334	  The homotopy is built directly from the away-localisation identification, \emph{not}
1335	  routed through \(\operatorname{SimplicialObject.Augmented.ExtraDegeneracy}\): the
1336	  extra-degeneracy packaging of the contracting homotopy is a documented dead end for
1337	  this cover (the prepend-\(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\) map is only a section-level homotopy
1338	  after localisation, not an augmented-simplicial extra degeneracy), so the concrete
1339	  \(\operatorname{depHomotopy}\)/\(\operatorname{depDiff}\) route of the dependent port
1340	  is used instead.
1341	\end{proof}

1343	\subsection{Presheaf-level {\v C}ech machinery}
1344	\label{sec:presheaf_cech_machinery}
1345	
1346	The {\v C}ech-acyclicity of injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1347	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} below) is not a statement about a single
1348	affine cover: it is proved on the abelian category \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\)
1349	of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, where the {\v C}ech complex of sections
1350	is the \(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex of an explicit free resolution. The argument
1351	rests on two presheaf-level facts, recorded here, and one transfer of injectivity.
1352	First, a free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) of presheaves of modules whose
1353	\(\operatorname{Hom}\)-complex into \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{is} the {\v C}ech complex
1354	of sections (Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex},
1355	Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and
1356	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}). Second, the resolution property of
1357	that complex: \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) resolves \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}\)
1358	concentrated in degree \(0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso}). Together
1359	these give: an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is injective as a presheaf
1360	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} via
1361	Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_of_adjoint} applied to the sheafification adjunction
1362	Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}), so \(\operatorname{Hom}(-, \mathcal{I})\) is
1363	exact and carries the augmented resolution to an exact section complex, i.e.\
1364	\(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\). This direct route uses
1365	neither enough-injectives in \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) nor a right-derived
1366	\(\delta\)-functor identification.
1367	
1368	\begin{definition}
1369	
1370	[Free presheaf complex of a cover]
1371	  \label{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1372	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex,
1373	    AlgebraicGeometry.freeYoneda,
1374	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverOpen,
1375	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen,
1376	    AlgebraicGeometry.coverInterOpen_comp_le,
1377	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreeSimplicial,
1378	    AlgebraicGeometry.cechFreePresheafComplex_X,
1379	    AlgebraicGeometry.sigma_ι_eqToHom_transport}
1380	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into}
1381	  % (the complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$) (read from
1382	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1142--1162, with the $j_{p!}$ description
1383	  % L1095--1135).
1384	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider the chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$
1385	  % of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1386	  % $$
1387	  % \ldots
1388	  % \to
1389	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1i_2} (j_{i_0i_1i_2})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1i_2}}
1390	  % \to
1391	  % \bigoplus_{i_0i_1} (j_{i_0i_1})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0i_1}}
1392	  % \to
1393	  % \bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}}
1394	  % \to 0 \to \ldots
1395	  % $$
1396	  % where the last nonzero term is placed in degree $0$
1397	  % and where the map
1398	  % $$
1399	  % (j_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1400	  % \longrightarrow
1401	  % (j_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}})_{p!}
1402	  % \mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0\ldots \hat i_j \ldots i_{p + 1}}}
1403	  % $$
1404	  % is given by $(-1)^j$ times the canonical map."
1405	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology,
1406	  \texttt{lemma-cech-map-into} (the free complex \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\)).}
1407	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an
1408	  open covering, with \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\)
1409	  the open immersion of each finite intersection. The Stacks formulation uses, for
1410	  each intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\), the \emph{extension-by-zero} presheaf
1411	  \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{!}\,\mathcal{O}_X|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\), the presheaf of
1412	  modules sending an open \(W\) to \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when
1413	  \(W \subset U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) and to \(0\) otherwise. This extension-by-zero
1414	  presheaf is, canonically, the \emph{free presheaf of modules on the representable}
1415	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\): writing
1416	  \(\mathbf{y}\,V = \operatorname{Hom}(-, V)\) for the Yoneda presheaf of the open
1417	  \(V\) on the site of opens of \(X\), the free presheaf of modules
1418	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V)\) sends \(W\) to the free
1419	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\)-module on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(W, V)\), which is
1420	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(W)\) if \(W \subset V\) and \(0\) otherwise — exactly
1421	  extension-by-zero of \(\mathcal{O}_X|_V\). Its defining universal property
1422	  \[
1423	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
1424	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,V),\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
1425	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{F}(V)
1426	  \]
1427	  is the free–forgetful adjunction for presheaves of modules composed with the
1428	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, G) \cong G(V)\). The
1429	  \emph{free presheaf complex} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the chain complex
1430	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) in the abelian category
1431	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose
1432	  term in degree \(p\) is the direct sum
1433	  \[
1434	    K(\mathcal{U})_p
1435	      = \bigoplus_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1436	        \operatorname{free}\bigl(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1437	  \]
1438	  with \(K(\mathcal{U})_p\) placed in degree \(p \geq 0\) (and \(0\) in negative
1439	  degrees), and whose differential \(K(\mathcal{U})_{p+1} \to K(\mathcal{U})_p\) is
1440	  the alternating sum \(\sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\) of the maps
1441	  \(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to
1442	  \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}})\)
1443	  obtained by applying \(\operatorname{free}\) to the representable index-dropping
1444	  maps \(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \to \mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1445	  \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\) induced by the inclusions of opens
1446	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} \subset U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}\)
1447	  that drop the index \(i_j\). Each summand is a \emph{projective} object of
1448	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\), because the universal property above identifies
1449	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots
1450	  i_p}), -)\) with the exact ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1451	  \((-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\); this is the structural feature exploited below.
1452	
1453	  We assume the index set \(I\) is \emph{finite} (so the required coproducts exist in
1454	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\); this matches the finiteness hypothesis of the
1455	  comparison target). Here the direct sum \(\bigoplus\) is the categorical coproduct
1456	  \(\coprod\) (the indexed \(\operatorname{Sigma}\) colimit), which for a finite index
1457	  set coincides with the finite biproduct.
1458	\end{definition}

1492	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of presheaves.}
1493	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1494	  covering. For a presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), the
1495	  \emph{section {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1496	  \mathcal{F})\) is the cochain complex of \emph{abelian groups} whose term in
1497	  degree \(p\) is the product of sections over the \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
1498	  \[
1499	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
1500	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
1501	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
1502	  \]
1503	  each factor being the value of the ``sections over \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)'' functor
1504	  (the evaluation of \(\mathcal{F}\) at the open \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\)), with
1505	  differential the alternating sum of restriction maps
1506	  \[
1507	    d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}
1508	      = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
1509	        s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}
1510	        \big|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}.
1511	  \]
1512	  This object is \emph{distinct} from the relative {\v C}ech complex
1513	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1514	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}: the latter is the pushforward complex with terms
1515	  \(\prod (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\) in
1516	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\), an object of relative sheaves over the base, whereas the
1517	  section {\v C}ech complex here is the simpler complex of \emph{global sections} of
1518	  \(\mathcal{F}\) over the intersections, an object of abelian groups (the underlying
1519	  additive structure of the sections). The two must not be conflated: the
1520	  presheaf-level acyclicity argument below runs entirely on this section complex.
1521	\end{definition}

1592	  Yoneda isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbf{y}\,V, \mathcal{F}) \cong
1593	  \mathcal{F}(V)\), the natural identification, for each multi-index,
1594	  \[

1597	    \bigr)

1602	    \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}),
1603	  \]

1605	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). Taking the product over multi-indices,

1607	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) =

1612	  \ldots i_{p+1}}) \to \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j}
1613	  \ldots i_{p+1}})\) defining the differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet\) become

1616	  complexes coincide; naturality in \(\mathcal{F}\) is immediate from the

1619	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}), \mathcal{F}) \cong \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) is additive,

1623	  the complex isomorphism by matching components degree by degree.

1649	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}.}
1650	  Let \(X\) be a ringed space and \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) an open
1651	  covering. The \emph{cover structure presheaf} \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}} :
1652	  X.\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}\) is the image presheaf of the augmentation
1653	  \[
1654	    \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i) \;\longrightarrow\; \mathbf{1},
1655	  \]
1656	  where the target \(\mathbf{1} = \operatorname{PresheafOfModules.unit}(
1657	  X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\) is the structure presheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\)
1658	  regarded as a presheaf of modules over itself. Concretely
1659	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)\) when \(W\) is contained in some
1660	  \(U_i\), and \(0\) otherwise. The degree-\(0\) term
1661	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_0 = \coprod_i \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_i)\) of the free

1668	[Objectwise detection of quasi-isomorphisms of presheaf-of-module complexes]
1669	  \label{lem:quasiIso_of_evaluation}
1670	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quasiIso_of_evaluation,
1671	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_Fmap_homologyMap,
1672	    AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_of_evaluation}
1673	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1674	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Lemma \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1675	  % (proof, the sectionwise-reduction step) (read from
1676	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1220--1226).

1678	  % complex over any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex."
1679	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, \texttt{lemma-homology-complex}
1680	  (sectionwise reduction).}
1681	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of rings on a category \(C\) and let
1682	  \(\varphi : K \to L\) be a morphism of chain complexes in the abelian category
1683	  \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules. Suppose that for \emph{every}
1684	  object \(V\) the evaluated morphism — the image of \(\varphi\) under the functor
1685	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\bigr).
1686	  \operatorname{mapHomologicalComplex}\), a morphism of complexes of
1687	  \(R(V)\)-modules — is a quasi-isomorphism. Then \(\varphi\) is a
1688	  quasi-isomorphism. This is the objectwise-reduction step that turns the
1689	  resolution claim of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_free_complex_quasi_iso} into a family of
1690	  sectionwise (combinatorial) statements: homology in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) is
1691	  computed open-by-open, because the evaluation functors
1692	  \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) are exact (hence preserve
1693	  homology) and jointly conservative. The Lean entry point is
1694	  \(\operatorname{quasiIso\_of\_evaluation}\), which combines the joint
1695	  conservativity of the evaluations (each \(g\) is an isomorphism as soon as every
1696	  \((\operatorname{evaluation} R\, V).\operatorname{map} g\) is) with the fact that
1697	  each \(\operatorname{PresheafOfModules.evaluation} R\, V\) preserves homology.
1698	\end{lemma}

1700	\begin{proof}
1701	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}
1702	  A morphism of complexes is a quasi-isomorphism iff it induces an isomorphism on
1703	  homology in every degree (\(\operatorname{quasiIso\_iff}\),
1704	  \(\operatorname{quasiIsoAt\_iff\_isIso\_homologyMap}\)). Fix a degree \(i\). The
1705	  homology map \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism in \(\mathrm{PMod}(R)\) iff each of
1706	  its evaluations is an isomorphism, since the underlying
1707	  presheaf-of-abelian-groups functor reflects isomorphisms and a morphism of
1708	  presheaves is an isomorphism iff it is so objectwise (joint conservativity). For
1709	  each \(V\), evaluation preserves homology, so the evaluation of \(H_i(\varphi)\)
1710	  is the homology map of the evaluated complex morphism; the latter is an
1711	  isomorphism because the evaluated morphism is a quasi-isomorphism by hypothesis.
1712	  Hence \(H_i(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), i.e.\ \(\varphi\) is a
1713	  quasi-isomorphism.
1714	\end{proof}

1736	  for the set of cover members containing \(V\). Evaluating the free presheaf complex

1738	  \(V\) gives, degreewise,
1739	  \[

1741	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I}
1742	        \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U_\sigma)(V)
1743	      = \bigoplus_{\substack{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I \\ V \leq U_\sigma}}
1744	        \mathcal{O}_X(V)
1745	      = \bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V),

1748	  \(V \leq W\) and \(0\) otherwise (poset hom-set is a subsingleton), and
1749	  \(V \leq U_\sigma = U_{\sigma(0)} \cap \cdots \cap U_{\sigma(p)}\) holds iff
1750	  \(\sigma\) takes all its values in \(I_1\). Under this identification the
1751	  differential of \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet(V)\) is the alternating-sum combinatorial
1752	  {\v C}ech differential of the full simplex on the index set \(I_1\) with
1753	  coefficients in the single module \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\). Moreover the evaluated
1754	  augmentation target is \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = \mathcal{O}_X(V)\) when
1755	  \(I_1 \neq \varnothing\) and \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}(V) = 0\) when
1756	  \(I_1 = \varnothing\). Thus the evaluation at \(V\) of the augmented complex
1757	  \(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathcal{U}}[0]\)
1758	  (\(\operatorname{cechFreeComplexAug}\)) is precisely the augmented combinatorial
1759	  {\v C}ech complex of the simplex on \(I_1\) with coefficients \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\).

1761	  \(\operatorname{cechFreePresheafComplex\_X}\) (which exposes

1773	  surviving \(\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1\) gives
1774	  \(\bigoplus_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(V)\).

1781	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}: it sends \(V\) to the free

3165	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-vanish-basis}
3166	% hold. Note that the intersection of standard opens in an affine is
3167	% another standard open. Hence property (1) holds.
3168	% The coverings form a cofinal system of open coverings of any element
3169	% of $\mathcal{B}$, see
3170	% Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-standard-open}.
3171	% Hence (2) holds.
3172	% Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
3173	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."
3174	\begin{proof}
3175	  \uses{def:affine_cover_system, lem:affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh,
3176	    lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis, def:absolute_cohomology}
3177	  The proof instantiates the basis-comparison criterion
3178	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) at the affine cover system. Assemble the affine cover
3179	  system \(s\) of Definition~\ref{def:affine_cover_system}: its basis \(\mathcal{B}\) is the
3180	  standard (distinguished) opens of the affine \(U\) and its admissible coverings \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are
3181	  the standard open covers, the three nontrivial fields being discharged by
3182	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:affine_faces_mem}, \ref{lem:affine_surj_of_vanishing}
3183	  and~\ref{lem:affine_injective_acyclic}.
3184	
3185	  For the quasi-coherent coefficient \(\mathcal{F}\), Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh}
3186	  supplies the seed: \(\mathcal{F}\) has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(s\)
3187	  (Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}), i.e.\ condition (3) of
3188	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis} holds for \(\mathcal{F}\). Applying the
3189	  basis-comparison lemma to \(s\) and \(\mathcal{F}\) yields, for every standard open
3190	  \(V \in \mathcal{B}\) and every \(p > 0\), the vanishing \(H^p(V, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Taking
3191	  \(V = U\) (the whole affine, itself a standard open) gives \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all
3192	  \(p > 0\). Throughout, the sheaf cohomology \(H^p(U, -) = \operatorname{Ext}^p(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, -)\)
3193	  is the Ext realization of Definition~\ref{def:absolute_cohomology}.
3194	\end{proof}

3196	\subsection{The affine instantiation (Tag 02KG)}
3197	
3198	The Serre vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} is obtained by feeding a concrete
3199	\emph{affine} cover system into the basis-comparison criterion. We construct that cover system
3200	(Definition~\ref{def:affine_cover_system}) and discharge its three nontrivial fields, then supply the
3201	quasi-coherent seed that meets condition (3). The sub-lemmas below are the formalize-ready cut points
3202	of this instantiation.
3203	
3204	\begin{lemma}
3205	[Distinguished opens are closed under finite intersection]
3206	  \label{lem:affine_faces_mem}
3207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affine_faces_mem}
3208	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover}
3209	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-standard-open}
3210	  % (surrounding prose on standard opens) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex,
3211	  % L513--517).
3212	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that $\Spec(R)$ has a basis of open sets $D(f)$,
3213	  % $f \in R$ which we call standard opens, see Algebra,
3214	  % Definition \ref{algebra-definition-Zariski-topology}.
3215	  % In addition, the intersection of two standard opens is another:
3216	  % $D(f) \cap D(g) = D(fg)$, $f, g\in R$."
3217	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-standard-open}
3218	  (standard opens).}
3219	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) the associated affine scheme. The
3220	  distinguished opens \(D(f)\), \(f \in R\), form a basis closed under intersection: \(D(f) \cap D(g)
3221	  = D(fg)\). Consequently, for any standard open cover \(\mathcal{U} : V = \bigcup_{i \in \iota}
3222	  D(g_i)\) of a distinguished open \(V\), every finite intersection \(D(g_{i_0}) \cap \cdots \cap
3223	  D(g_{i_p}) = D(g_{i_0} \cdots g_{i_p})\) of its members is again a distinguished open. This is the
3224	  \(\mathrm{faces\_mem}\) field (condition (1)) of the affine cover system.
3225	\end{lemma}
3226	\begin{proof}
3227	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover}
3228	  The identity \(D(f) \cap D(g) = D(fg)\) holds because a prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) avoids both \(f\) and
3229	  \(g\) iff it avoids the product \(fg\). Iterating over the indices \(i_0, \ldots, i_p\) of a face
3230	  gives \(\bigcap_k D(g_{i_k}) = D\bigl(\prod_k g_{i_k}\bigr)\), a distinguished open; the base set
3231	  \(V = D(f)\) is distinguished by hypothesis. Hence every face of a standard cover lies in the basis.
3232	\end{proof}

3323	\begin{lemma}
3324	[Covering-datum bridge to the open-cover {\v C}ech form]
3325	  \label{lem:cover_datum_bridge}
3326	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverDatum_bridge}
3327	  \uses{def:basis_cov_system}
3328	  \textit{Project-bespoke compatibility lemma.}
3329	  The injective {\v C}ech-acyclicity Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} is stated over an
3330	  open-cover object with a finiteness hypothesis on the index set, whereas the affine cover system's
3331	  admissible coverings are raw covering data \((\iota, U)\) (\(\mathrm{CovDatum}\), with \(\iota\)
3332	  finite, of Definition~\ref{def:basis_cov_system}). This lemma exhibits, for each finite standard
3333	  covering datum, the canonical identification of its section {\v C}ech complex with the section
3334	  {\v C}ech complex of the corresponding open-cover object, so that the {\v C}ech cohomology computed
3335	  in the two presentations agrees.
3336	\end{lemma}
3337	\begin{proof}
3338	  \uses{def:basis_cov_system}
3339	  A standard covering datum \((\iota, U)\) with \(\iota\) finite is precisely the data of a finite
3340	  family of opens \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) whose union is the covered open; this is the
3341	  same underlying data as the open-cover object carried by
3342	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}. The degreewise products
3343	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(V_\sigma)\) defining the two section {\v C}ech complexes range over the
3344	  same index tuples and use the same face opens \(V_\sigma\), and the coface maps coincide; hence the
3345	  two complexes are identified and their cohomologies agree.
3346	\end{proof}

3415	  % (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691--719).

3429	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Writing \(M = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global sections,
3430	  there is a natural isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{M}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
3431	  under which \(\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F}) = M_f\) for every \(f \in R\); in particular the sections of

3440	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and \(R\)-modules; the counit
3441	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})} \to \mathcal{F}\) is an isomorphism for quasi-coherent
3442	  \(\mathcal{F}\). By Tag 01HV the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over \(D(f)\) are \(M_f\), so the
3443	  isomorphism identifies \(\Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) with \(M_f\).

3447	[Standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing for quasi-coherent coefficients]
3448	  \label{lem:affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh}
3449	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh}
3450	  \uses{lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections, def:affine_cover_system,
3451	    def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}
3452	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KG, Lemma
3453	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} (proof, condition (3)) (read
3454	  % from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L172--173).
3455	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Finally, condition (3) of the lemma follows from
3456	  % Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}."

3458	  (\texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero}, proof).}
3459	  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\) be an affine scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
3460	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then \(\mathcal{F}\) has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for the
3461	  affine cover system (Definition~\ref{def:affine_cover_system},
3462	  Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}): for every standard cover \(\mathcal{U}\) and every
3463	  \(p > 0\) one has \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). This is the seed that supplies
3464	  condition (3) of the basis-comparison Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}, and is the 01I8
3465	  globalisation of the tilde-case standard-cover vanishing.

3470	  \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\), with sections over each face \(D(g_\sigma)\)
3471	  the away localisation \(M_{g_\sigma}\). The standard-cover {\v C}ech vanishing of
3472	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} applies to the tilde sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\): its section
3473	  {\v C}ech complex over any standard cover is exact in positive degrees. Transporting along the
3474	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{M}\), which is natural in the coefficient and so induces
3475	  an isomorphism of section {\v C}ech complexes, gives \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\)
3476	  for all \(p > 0\) and every standard cover \(\mathcal{U}\). Hence \(\mathcal{F}\) has vanishing
3477	  higher {\v C}ech cohomology for the affine cover system.

3480	The {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis
3481	(Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) is proved by a dimension shift on the
3482	coefficient module, decomposed below into four sub-lemmas: the short exact sequence of
3483	{\v C}ech complexes coming from a basis (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}), the
3484	resulting stability of vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology under the injective
3485	quotient (Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}), the sheaf-cohomology base case
3486	\(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing}), and the
3487	dimension-shift induction \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\)
3488	(Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}). The top lemma's proof then just
3489	chains these.

3491	\subsection{Section {\v C}ech infrastructure and the cover-system encoding}
3492	
3493	The sub-lemmas below are formalized in a \emph{cover-local}, presheaf-level, hypothesis-driven
3494	form: each lemma fixes a single index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) and works with the
3495	section {\v C}ech complexes of that one covering, taking the geometric inputs (section-level
3496	exactness on faces, positive-degree {\v C}ech vanishing) as hypotheses. The cover-global
3497	\((\mathcal{B}, \mathrm{Cov})\) data of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis} re-enters only
3498	in the inductive step (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}), where it is packaged by
3499	the cover-system encoding (Definition~\ref{def:basis_cov_system},
3500	Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}) and discharges those hypotheses via the per-face
3501	short-exact-sequence derivation (Lemma~\ref{lem:face_ses_of_sheaf_ses}). The supporting
3502	definitions and the degreewise product engine are collected first.

3504	\begin{definition}
3505	
3506	\leanok
3507	[{\v C}ech cohomology accessor]
3508	  \label{def:cech_cohomology_accessor}
3509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechCohomology}
3510	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex}
3511	  \textit{Project-local accessor.}
3512	  For an index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) (giving the covering \(\mathcal{U}\)), a
3513	  presheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(p \geq 0\), the \emph{{\v C}ech
3514	  cohomology} \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the degree-\(p\) homology of the section
3515	  {\v C}ech complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex},
3516	  \[
3517	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\;
3518	      H^p\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr),
3519	  \]
3520	  taken as an object of \(\mathrm{Ab}\). This is the named wrapper that the 01EO chain refers to
3521	  uniformly.
3522	\end{definition}
3523	
3524	\begin{definition}
3525	[Functoriality of the section {\v C}ech complex]
3526	  \label{def:section_cech_functoriality}
3527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCosimplicialMap,
3528	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechCosimplicialFunctor,
3529	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplexFunctor,
3530	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplexMap}
3531	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex}
3532	  \textit{Project-local.}
3533	  A morphism \(\varphi : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}\) of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
3534	  acts on the section {\v C}ech data of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} coordinatewise:
3535	  on the basic-open section group of each intersection it is the map
3536	  \(\varphi(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) : \mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}) \to \mathcal{G}(U_{i_0 \ldots
3537	  i_p})\). These assemble into a cosimplicial morphism (the \emph{section {\v C}ech cosimplicial
3538	  map}), functorial in \(\varphi\) (the \emph{section {\v C}ech cosimplicial functor}). Composing
3539	  with the alternating-coface-map complex functor packages the section {\v C}ech complex as a
3540	  functor in the coefficient presheaf (the \emph{section {\v C}ech complex functor}) and yields,
3541	  for each \(\varphi\), the induced chain map of section {\v C}ech complexes
3542	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \varphi)\). These are the maps used to build the short
3543	  complex of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_short_complex}.
3544	\end{definition}
3545	
3546	\begin{definition}
3547	
3548	\leanok
3549	[Per-face section short complex]
3550	  \label{def:face_short_complex}
3551	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.faceShortComplex}
3552	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex}
3553	  \textit{Project-local building block.}
3554	  Let \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) be an index family and let
3555	  \(P : \mathcal{P}_1 \to \mathcal{P}_2 \to \mathcal{P}_3\) be a short complex of presheaves of
3556	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. For a degree \(p\) and a tuple \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\)
3557	  write \(V_\sigma = \bigcap_k U(\sigma k)\) for the corresponding basic open. The \emph{per-face
3558	  short complex} \(\mathrm{face}(U, P, \sigma)\) is the short complex of abelian groups obtained by
3559	  evaluating \(P\) on sections over \(V_\sigma\),
3560	  \[
3561	    \mathcal{P}_1(V_\sigma) \longrightarrow \mathcal{P}_2(V_\sigma) \longrightarrow
3562	      \mathcal{P}_3(V_\sigma),
3563	  \]
3564	  the maps being the section-level actions over \(V_\sigma\) of the two maps of \(P\). It is the
3565	  \((p, \sigma)\)-indexed factor whose product over all tuples \(\sigma\) is the degree-\(p\) term
3566	  of the section {\v C}ech complex.
3567	\end{definition}
3568	
3569	\begin{definition}
3570	
3571	\leanok
3572	[Short complex of section {\v C}ech complexes]
3573	  \label{def:section_cech_short_complex}
3574	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCechComplexShortComplex}
3575	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:section_cech_functoriality}
3576	  \textit{Project-local.}
3577	  For an index family \(U\) and a short complex
3578	  \(P : \mathcal{P}_1 \to \mathcal{P}_2 \to \mathcal{P}_3\) of presheaves of
3579	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the \emph{short complex of section {\v C}ech complexes} is
3580	  \[
3581	    0 \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_1) \to
3582	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_2) \to
3583	      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{P}_3) \to 0,
3584	  \]
3585	  a short complex in the category of cochain complexes of abelian groups, with the two maps the
3586	  chain maps induced by the maps of \(P\) through the functoriality of
3587	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_functoriality}. Its short-exactness is the conclusion of
3588	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis} and the input to Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}.
3589	\end{definition}

3593	\leanok

3598	  \textit{Project-bespoke encoding of the hypotheses of Stacks 01EO.}
3599	  A \emph{cover system on a basis} for a scheme \(X\) is a record with five fields. A
3600	  \emph{covering datum} (the helper \(\mathrm{CovDatum}\)) is a pair \((\iota, U)\) of an index type
3601	  \(\iota\) together with an index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\); it is the raw shape
3602	  \(\Sigma\,\iota,\ \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) over which the section {\v C}ech complex is built.
3603	  The cover system bundles:
3604	  \begin{enumerate}

3600	  \emph{covering datum} (the helper \(\mathrm{CovDatum}\)) is a pair \((\iota, U)\) of an index type
3601	  \(\iota\) together with an index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\); it is the raw shape
3602	  \(\Sigma\,\iota,\ \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) over which the section {\v C}ech complex is built.
3603	  The cover system bundles:
3604	  \begin{enumerate}
3605	  \item a basis \(\mathcal{B} \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) for the topology;
3606	  \item a set \(\mathrm{Cov}\) of admissible coverings, each a \(\mathrm{CovDatum}\);
3607	  \item \emph{faces-in-basis} (\(\mathrm{faces\_mem}\)): for every covering in \(\mathrm{Cov}\) every
3608	    finite intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) of its opens lies in \(\mathcal{B}\) (condition (1) of
3609	    Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis});
3610	  \item \emph{section surjectivity} (\(\mathrm{surj\_of\_vanishing}\)): for every \(V \in \mathcal{B}\)
3611	    and every short exact sequence \(S : 0 \to S_1 \to S_2 \to S_3 \to 0\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
3612	    whose left term \(S_1\) has vanishing positive {\v C}ech cohomology over the coverings of \(V\) in
3613	    \(\mathrm{Cov}\), the section map \(S_2(V) \to S_3(V)\) is surjective;
3614	  \item \emph{injective acyclicity} (\(\mathrm{injective\_acyclic}\)): for every injective
3615	    \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\) and every covering \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) the
3616	    positive-degree {\v C}ech cohomology vanishes, \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for
3617	    all \(p > 0\).
3618	  \end{enumerate}
3619	  Unlike a bare combinatorial record, the structure carries two sheaf-theoretic ({\v C}ech-cohomology)
3620	  fields. The field \(\mathrm{surj\_of\_vanishing}\) is \emph{not} the raw cofinality datum: it is the
3621	  section-surjectivity \emph{output} that cofinality together with Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1}
3622	  produces, packaged directly as the consequence the 01EO chain consumes. The field
3623	  \(\mathrm{injective\_acyclic}\) is the injective {\v C}ech-acyclicity of
3624	  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} for the system's own coverings. These are exactly the data
3625	  that the dimension-shift induction Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing} consumes.
3626	\end{definition}

3628	\begin{definition}
3629	
3630	\leanok
3631	[Vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for a cover system]
3632	  \label{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}
3633	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.HasVanishingHigherCech}
3634	  \uses{def:basis_cov_system, def:cech_cohomology_accessor}
3635	  \textit{Project-bespoke inductive predicate (condition (3) of Stacks 01EO, per module).}
3636	  Given a cover system \(s\) on a basis (Definition~\ref{def:basis_cov_system}), an
3637	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for
3638	  \(s\)} when
3639	  \[
3640	    \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0 \qquad
3641	      \text{for every } \mathcal{U} \in s.\mathrm{Cov} \text{ and every } p > 0,
3642	  \]
3643	  with \(\check{H}^p\) the accessor of Definition~\ref{def:cech_cohomology_accessor}. This is the
3644	  per-module form of condition (3). It is deliberately stated for an \emph{arbitrary}
3645	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module: the dimension-shift induction of
3646	  Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing} feeds the quotient \(\mathcal{Q} =
3647	  \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\) back as the coefficient, and \(\mathcal{Q}\) need not be quasi-coherent
3648	  even when \(\mathcal{F}\) is. The inductive class is therefore ``\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules with
3649	  vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(s\)'', a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) entering only
3650	  as the seed at the affine (Tag 02KG) instantiation; the predicate must \emph{not} be specialized
3651	  to quasi-coherent modules.
3652	\end{definition}
